w12.title: Floor care — traditional vacuum vs. robot vacuum
w12.topic: Floor care — traditional vacuum vs. robot vacuum

tech.body: Students compare energy consumption, cleaning effectiveness, and convenience of different vacuum types.

math.body: Students calculate annual energy costs and lifecycle costs including purchase price and replacement parts.

env.body: Students understand that “convenience” can increase energy use if devices run unnecessarily.

green.body: Students evaluate the trade-offs between cleaning performance and energy use.

w12.resources: Calculator, electricity tariff (e.g. €0.30/kWh), product specifications from manufacturer websites or appliance labels.
w12.intro: Keeping floors clean is a daily or weekly household task. The choice of vacuum cleaner has changed dramatically — where once only corded upright or cylinder machines existed, robotic vacuums now navigate rooms autonomously. But which option uses more energy? The answer depends not only on the motor power but also on how often and how long the device runs. In this worksheet you will calculate and compare the true energy cost of floor care.

theory.title: Theory

trad.label: Traditional vacuum cleaners:
trad.body: use a motor of typically 700–2400 W for corded models. Since 2014, EU Regulation (EU) No 666/2013 has capped rated power at 900 W. Higher wattage does not necessarily mean better suction — filter condition and nozzle design matter more. A typical cleaning session lasts 30–60 minutes.

robot.label: Robotic vacuums:
robot.body: use a much smaller motor, typically 20–100 W, but run for longer sessions (60–150 minutes) and often run daily rather than weekly. They also draw power when docked for charging (typically 3–25 W standby). Some models run at night.

freq.label: Cleaning frequency
freq.body: matters enormously for energy totals. A traditional vacuum used once a week uses far less energy per year than a robotic vacuum running every day, even if its power is lower.

theory.label: EU energy labels for vacuum cleaners show:
theory.label2: annual energy use (kWh, based on 50 cleaning cycles per year),
theory.label5: cleaning performance on carpet and hard floors.

worked.title: Worked example
worked.tradTitle: Traditional vacuum: 900 W, 45 min per session, once a week = 52 sessions/year.
worked.robotTitle: Robotic vacuum: 35 W, runs 90 min/day, 7 days/week = 365 sessions/year. Standby: 8 W for 22.5 h/day.
worked.robotL3: Robotic total: 84.9 kWh — much more than the traditional vacuum!

act1.intro: Fill in values from appliance labels or manufacturer websites, or use the defaults provided.
act1.q1: For each appliance, calculate the annual energy use in kWh (including standby energy for the robotic vacuum).
act1.q2: Calculate the annual electricity cost (€) for each at €0.30/kWh. Which is most expensive to run?
act1.q3: If the robotic vacuum ran only 4 days per week instead of 7, how much would the annual energy use change?
act1.q4: What type of battery is typically used (Li-ion, NiMH)? How many charge cycles before replacement? Calculate the annual cost of battery replacement.

act2.intro: “Vampire power” or “phantom load” is electricity consumed by devices when not in active use — charging on a dock, waiting in standby, or displaying a status light.
act2.q1: Calculate the annual standby energy of the robotic vacuum (8 W × 22.5 h/day × 365 days). What percentage of its total annual energy is this?
act2.q2: A smart power strip could cut standby to zero. How much money per year would this save? Is it worth paying €15 for the strip?
act2.q3: Research: find two other household devices with significant standby consumption. Estimate their annual standby energy.

act3.q1: Calculate the 10-year total cost for each vacuum (purchase + energy + parts). Assume you buy a second robot after 5 years.

act4.intro: Traditional vacuum (900 W, 45 min weekly) vs. robot vacuum (35 W, 90 min daily + standby).
act4.q1: Calculate annual CO₂ emissions for both (0.27 kg CO₂/kWh). How many times more CO₂ does the robot vacuum emit?
act4.q2: If the robot runs only 4 days instead of 7 per week, what is the annual saving in kg CO₂?

peer.title: Peer-to-peer tip
peer.dykBody: Empty the robot’s bin regularly — a full bin reduces suction and makes the robot run longer.
peer.q: How does that affect energy use?

refl.q1: Consider a student living in a small flat who cleans once a week. Which vacuum type would you recommend and why? Use energy and practical arguments (noise, convenience, purchase cost).
refl.q2: Does a robot vacuum encourage people to clean more often? If a home was previously vacuumed once a week but the robot runs daily, does that increase or decrease total energy use? Justify.
refl.q3: Would you recommend a robot vacuum for a small apartment (40 m²) or a large house (150 m²)? Explain using energy and cost arguments.
refl.q4: How would installing photovoltaic panels on the school roof affect this carbon footprint calculation?

ee.title: Additional tasks — for electrical engineering students
ee.intro: These additional questions are aimed at students enrolled in electrical or electronic engineering programmes.
ee.q1: Draw a block diagram of a traditional vacuum cleaner (major components); indicate typical power ratings of each component.
ee.q2: Why does a vacuum cleaner have a low power factor (cos φ ≈ 0.7–0.8)? How does this affect the electricity bill?
ee.q3: If a vacuum draws 900 W at 230 V with cos φ = 0.75, calculate the current (I = P/(V × cos φ)).
ee.q4: A vacuum cleaner has 900 W electrical input but only 150 W of suction power (air watts). Calculate the efficiency. Where does the rest of the energy go?
ee.q5: Draw a block diagram of a robot vacuum (major components); indicate typical power ratings.
ee.q6: What type of motor does a robot vacuum use (brushed DC, BLDC) and why? Discuss BLDC advantages (efficiency, noise, lifetime) and disadvantages (cost, control electronics).
